summary: Martin Fowler critiques Bimodal IT, a Gartner-promoted approach that divides systems into fast-moving 'systems of engagement' and slow, reliable 'systems of record.' He argues this distinction is fundamentally flawed for two reasons: first, rapid innovation in front-office systems inevitably requires changes to back-office systems too. Second, the approach falsely assumes quality must be traded for speed, when in fact Thoughtworks' experience shows high quality enables faster delivery with fewer defects. Fowler recommends instead organizing around business capabilities and distinguishing between utility and strategic functions.
key_points: Bimodal IT wrongly separates systems by technical role rather than business activity, ignoring that back-office changes are needed to support front-office innovation
The approach rests on the false premise that quality can be traded for speed, when evidence shows high quality actually accelerates delivery
Thoughtworks achieved both rapid feature delivery and dramatically fewer production defects using agile approaches
Organizations should instead take a business-capability-centric approach, distinguishing utility from strategic capabilities
money_quotes: Bimodal IT is the flawed notion that software systems should be divided into these two distinct categories for management and control.
You can't come up with clever pricing plans without modifying the systems of record that support them.
The bimodal idea is founded on the TradableQualityHypothesis, the idea that quality is something you trade-off for speed. It's a common notion, but a false one.
High quality (and low defects) are a crucial enabler for rapid cycle-time.
By not paying attention to quality, people following a bimodal approach will actually end up slowing down their pace of innovation in their 'systems of engagement'.
I find it hard to resist calling it 'Bipolar IT.'
key_insight: Quality and speed are not trade-offs—high quality is actually a prerequisite for rapid innovation, making Bimodal IT's core premise fundamentally wrong.
